title: Lead single

infobox.value: (or **first single**)[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is the first [single](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)") to be released from a [studio album](/wiki/Studio_album "Studio album") by an [artist](/wiki/Musician "Musician") or a [band](/wiki/Musical_ensemble "Musical ensemble"), usually before the album itself is released and also occasionally on the same day of the album's release date.

["Which Beyoncé Lead Single is Your All-Time Favorite? Vote!"](https://www.billboard.com/music/music-news/beyonce-best-lead-single-poll-1235090236/). *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")*.

["!!! Announce new album 'Let It be Blue' with first single 'Storm Around the World'"](https://www.nme.com/en_asia/news/music/announce-new-album-let-it-be-blue-with-first-single-storm-around-the-world-3170584). *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")*. February 26, 2022.

["The 100 Greatest Debut Singles of All Time"](https://www.rollingstone.com/music/music-lists/greatest-debut-songs-singles-990470/). *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*. May 19, 2020.

["Exclusive: Fall Out Boy Album Deets"](https://web.archive.org/web/20141228124802/https://www.rollingstone.com/music/news/exclusive-fall-out-boy-album-deets-20061116). *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*. November 16, 2006. Archived from [the original](https://www.rollingstone.com/music/news/exclusive-fall-out-boy-album-deets-20061116) on December 28, 2014. Retrieved December 12, 2014.

Leight, Elias (May 6, 2018). ["Why Your Favorite Artist Is Releasing More Singles Than Ever"](https://www.rollingstone.com/music/music-features/why-your-favorite-artist-is-releasing-more-singles-than-ever-629130/). *Rolling Stone*. Retrieved September 28, 2019.

Dombal, Ryan (October 1, 2013). ["Justin Timberlake: *The 20/20 Experience 2 of 2*"](https://pitchfork.com/reviews/albums/18548-justin-timberlake-the-2020-experience-2-of-2). *Pitchfork*. Retrieved February 17, 2019.

Espinoza, Joshua (January 6, 2017). ["Ed Sheeran returns with two new tracks "Castle on the Hill" and "Shape of You""](https://www.complex.com/music/2017/01/ed-sheeran-castle-on-hill-shape-of-you-divide). *Complex*. Retrieved June 12, 2019.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

First single released from an album

A **lead single** (or **first single**)[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is the first [single](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)") to be released from a [studio album](/wiki/Studio_album "Studio album") by an [artist](/wiki/Musician "Musician") or a [band](/wiki/Musical_ensemble "Musical ensemble"), usually before the album itself is released and also occasionally on the same day of the album's release date.

A similar term, "debut single", is the first single released by a new artist.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Release strategies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lead_single&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Release strategies")]

Artists often choose songs that are more [up-tempo](/wiki/Up-tempo "Up-tempo"), yet representative of the album's sound, as lead singles. Such songs are often catchier and attract the attention of listeners. The subsequent single might then be slower in tempo, in order to demonstrate the range of the album. Female vocalists like [Mariah Carey](/wiki/Mariah_Carey "Mariah Carey") and [Christina Aguilera](/wiki/Christina_Aguilera "Christina Aguilera") often maintain a formula of an up-tempo first lead single with a slow [ballad](/wiki/Ballad "Ballad") follow-up. For example, two singles were released by [Miley Cyrus](/wiki/Miley_Cyrus "Miley Cyrus") before her album *[Bangerz](/wiki/Bangerz "Bangerz")* – an up-tempo track, "[We Can't Stop](/wiki/We_Can%27t_Stop "We Can't Stop")", was released as the first, and a slow-ballad song, "[Wrecking Ball](/wiki/Wrecking_Ball_(Miley_Cyrus_song) "Wrecking Ball (Miley Cyrus song)")", as the second. This was a successful practice of 1980s [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") bands. [Girls Aloud](/wiki/Girls_Aloud "Girls Aloud") chose to use "[The Show](/wiki/The_Show_(Girls_Aloud_song) "The Show (Girls Aloud song)")", a [dance-pop](/wiki/Dance-pop "Dance-pop") and [electropop](/wiki/Electropop "Electropop") song, as their lead single for their second album, *[What Will the Neighbours Say?](/wiki/What_Will_the_Neighbours_Say%3F "What Will the Neighbours Say?")*, following the fact that their previous singles from their debut album [*Sound of the Underground*](/wiki/Sound_of_the_Underground_(album) "Sound of the Underground (album)") were "quite down and moody"[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] according to band member [Cheryl](/wiki/Cheryl_Cole "Cheryl Cole"). However, not all artists decide to choose their lead single with the up-tempo criteria. Artists may release a lead single that has a message they want to convey to listeners instead of a song with more commercial potential, such as [Fall Out Boy](/wiki/Fall_Out_Boy "Fall Out Boy") who chose to release "[This Ain't a Scene, It's an Arms Race](/wiki/This_Ain%27t_a_Scene,_It%27s_an_Arms_Race "This Ain't a Scene, It's an Arms Race")" over the radio-friendly "[Thnks fr th Mmrs](/wiki/Thnks_fr_th_Mmrs "Thnks fr th Mmrs")".[[4]](#cite_note-RSDeets-4)

Japanese artists such as [Ayumi Hamasaki](/wiki/Ayumi_Hamasaki "Ayumi Hamasaki"), [Namie Amuro](/wiki/Namie_Amuro "Namie Amuro") and [B'z](/wiki/B%27z "B'z") may release four to eight singles before their albums to achieve record-breaking debut-week sales. The lead singles in Japan are very heavily advertised and promoted, in some cases even more than the album itself. With album sales continuously declining in the United States, record labels often release singles before the album's release date to online music retailers including [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes"), ranging in price from $0.99 to $1.29. This trend has become increasingly popular in many markets.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the late 2010s, artists began a trend of releasing multiple singles before eventually releasing a studio album. An unnamed A&R representative confirmed to *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* in 2018 that "an artist has
to build a foundation to sustain" and adding that "When artists have one big record and go run with that, it doesn't work because they never had a foundation to begin with." The same article cited examples such as [Cardi B](/wiki/Cardi_B "Cardi B"), [Camila Cabello](/wiki/Camila_Cabello "Camila Cabello") and [Jason Derulo](/wiki/Jason_Derulo "Jason Derulo") releasing four or more singles before their album releases.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Pre-release single

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lead_single&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-release single")]

In the 2000s, a common trend developed to release a lead single months in advance of the album release date. It has equally become common for further singles to be released before the release of the album. For example, [Usher](/wiki/Usher_(entertainer) "Usher (entertainer)") issued the lead single "[Love in This Club](/wiki/Love_in_This_Club "Love in This Club")" four months before the May 29, 2008, release of *[Here I Stand](/wiki/Here_I_Stand_(Usher_album) "Here I Stand (Usher album)")*. The second single "[Love in This Club Part II](/wiki/Love_in_This_Club_Part_II "Love in This Club Part II")" was released one month before the album release date. Another example is [Justin Timberlake](/wiki/Justin_Timberlake "Justin Timberlake")'s 2013 album, *[The 20/20 Experience](/wiki/The_20/20_Experience "The 20/20 Experience")*, which was released on March 19, 2013. The first single off it is "[Suit & Tie](/wiki/Suit_%26_Tie "Suit & Tie")", released two months before the album hit stores. One month before the release of the album, another single called "[Mirrors](/wiki/Mirrors_(Justin_Timberlake_song) "Mirrors (Justin Timberlake song)")" was released.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Katy Perry released "[California Gurls](/wiki/California_Gurls "California Gurls")" as a single on May 7, 2010, and "[Teenage Dream](/wiki/Teenage_Dream_(Katy_Perry_song) "Teenage Dream (Katy Perry song)")" on July 23, 2010, leading to the release of the album *[Teenage Dream](/wiki/Teenage_Dream_(Katy_Perry_album) "Teenage Dream (Katy Perry album)")* on August 24, 2010. [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran") did something similar, releasing both "[Shape of You](/wiki/Shape_of_You "Shape of You")" and "[Castle on the Hill](/wiki/Castle_on_the_Hill "Castle on the Hill")" as double lead singles from his album *[÷](/wiki/%C3%B7_(album) "÷ (album)")*, although these songs were both released on the same day, 6 January 2017.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The 1995 [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)") single "[Some Might Say](/wiki/Some_Might_Say "Some Might Say")" would go on to appear on the group's second album, *[(What's the Story) Morning Glory?](/wiki/(What%27s_the_Story)_Morning_Glory%3F "(What's the Story) Morning Glory?")*, though at the time it was a standalone release, with "[Roll with It](/wiki/Roll_with_It_(Oasis_song) "Roll with It (Oasis song)")" acting more as a lead single.

## Usage in South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lead_single&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Usage in South Korea")]

In [South Korean music](/wiki/South_Korean_music "South Korean music"), the term "**title track**" is sometimes used interchangeably with "lead single". The term is used to describe an album's main promoted song, usually released on the same day as the album and promoted on [South Korean music programs](/wiki/Music_programs_of_South_Korea "Music programs of South Korea").[[8]](#cite_note-cam-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) This term is used regardless of the song's title or whether or not it was the first single from an album to be released.[[8]](#cite_note-cam-8) For example, "[Boom Boom Bass](/wiki/Boom_Boom_Bass "Boom Boom Bass")" is considered the title track from [Riize](/wiki/Riize "Riize")'s 2024 [extended play](/wiki/EP "EP") *[Riizing](/wiki/Riizing "Riizing")*[[10]](#cite_note-10) despite not sharing a title with the EP, nor being the first single released from the album.
